title: Matins: The First Interrogation
subtitle: The Lord, bound, struck on the cheek before Annas in the dark of night

stillness: Be roused from your sleep at the hour of Matins.
Rise full of tears, worn out with grief,
for the things you pondered after Compline.
Now grow quiet, and within your spirit see.
gospel: The High Priest questioned Jesus about His disciples,
“I have spoken openly to the world.
I have always taught in the synagogue and in the temple,
where all the Jews come together,
and in secret I have said nothing.
Question those who have heard what I said to them.
Behold, they know what I have spoken.”
And when He had said this,
one of the officers standing by
gave Jesus a blow with his hand, saying:
“Is this how You answer the High Priest?”
“If I have spoken wrongly, bear witness of the wrong;
but if rightly, why do you strike Me?”
behold: Attend to each thing as though you were present.
Behold your Lord, seated among His enemies,
abandoned by His own disciples and friends,
bound and standing before Annas the High Priest.
See Him questioned in cunning, not in love of truth,
that they might find some falsehood to accuse Him.
See the officer—the one whose ear He had healed—
ungrateful for the kindness he received,
strike the cheek of his Healer.
Behold the blow fall upon that honorable Face,
upon which the angels long to gaze.
And the Lord, long-suffering and patient,
but bore this insult, as He would bear many more,
with all patience and gentleness, for us.
consider: What soul devoted to You, seeing and hearing this,
could hold back its tears, or hide its inward grief?
Chrysostom cries: one gave a blow to Jesus, who said such things—
what could be more senseless than this?
Let heaven shudder, let the earth be struck out of its mind,
at the Lord’s long-suffering and the servants’ contempt!
And Augustine: if we consider who received that blow,
would we not wish the one who struck Him
or swallowed by the gaping earth?
Yet what of these could He not have commanded by His power,
through whom the world was made—
had He not chosen rather to teach us the patience
by which the world is overcome?
He who fashioned the world bore the hand of a wretched man.
He bound Himself that He might loose us.
Learn from Him to bridle your own mouth,
that you speak nothing against the divine honor.
prayer: O Lord Jesus, how do You sit so despised and desolate?
Where are Your disciples and Your friends?
O my one good! O my one and only joy!
What shall I do, seeing You like this?
Surely, Lord, I will sit on the ground with You,
and keep You company,
for I see no one here who loves You,
but rather Your enemies, raging and mad, who hate You.
Lord Jesus Christ, who at the morning hour, before Annas,
received a blow from a servant,
and let Yourself be led, bound, to Caiaphas,
and through the whole night bore many insults and afflictions:
grant me, a sinner, I beseech You,
that I may rejoice to suffer the like for the glory of Your name,
and may deserve to enjoy those eternal joys.
With those same eyes of Your tender mercy,
with which You then looked upon Peter,
look often upon me, wretched as I am,
who so many times denied You
by evil thoughts, words, deeds, and neglects,
that I may come to know my faults and repent of them,
and deserve to find pardon and grace with You. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: O unicum bonum meum! O singulare gaudium meum! Quid faciam cum te sic videam?
latinJewel.english: O my one good! O my one and only joy! What shall I do, when I see You like this?
